Hi, welcome to the copywriting course. My name is Kevin Halbert. First off, I'd like to say I'm not a professional speaker, so this presentation I'm sure will be somewhat flawed. I'll have some ooms and some oz and I'll lose my train of thought and things of that nature. And sometimes I'll get off topic, but I'll get back on track eventually and we'll get a whole bunch of stuff covered. Finally I was going to write this copywriting course and create it based on writing emails. And the more I thought about it, the more I felt that to better serve you, I would give you a more complete foundation and some higher level techniques to use when writing copy. And then what I did is after I wrote all this stuff out and created this presentation that you're about to see, it's all going to be in this PowerPoint style, VSL, video sales presentation. And the reason I did that is because I think the text on screen with audio, as we know, is a very immersive and hypnotic technique for the viewer. And in this case, it's great because it'll help you absorb this stuff that much sooner and internalize it and get it into your DNA on a cellular level, as my father would say. So I really wanted to do it that way. So I wanted to do a much broader copywriting course. So like I said, we're going to give you all these components. And then after I got to the end of putting this presentation together with all these components, then I decided to write an ad based on using most of these components, not all of them, but use most of them. And when we go through this, you need to know why you're using these components. And when you know it, then you'll know when you should and shouldn't use certain techniques in your copy if it will fit what you're doing. So I'm going to cover that and you'll start to understand why we're doing the things we're doing and the way we're doing them. So first off, I'd like to say that whenever I'm talking to anybody about writing copy or they're writing copy or someone's asking about advice on copy and stuff like that, one of the first things I do is I imagine that I have a imaginary competitor. He's another guy who's trying to write copy and he's trying to write better copy than me for the exact same project. And so I just need to outsell him. That keeps me thinking, well, I could use this technique or that technique, but if I don't know what he's going to write and I need to win, what is it that I will bring out? What do I have in my arsenal that I can bring out to try to beat him when we both don't know what each other's going to write? And that's kind of the situation that you're in right now because I don't know what you're going to write. So I want to give you the most powerful tools you can use to give you the best odds on the most powerful and high converting copy that you can possibly write. And so this is something I think we'll get, you know, if you're running in and you had 10 jobs and they were for different clients and different products and services, this is what I think would win most of the time. So I'm going to teach you these very powerful techniques. So let's get started. Okay, one of the first things that a good copywriter does, and this is how you can tell if you have a good copywriter, is the type of questions he asks when he starts to embark on writing copy, especially for some new product or niche. And the first thing the copywriter should be asking himself and asking the client is, is there a sizable market for what they're selling? You know, and so that, you know, is what you're selling also. Okay, so there's a sizable market and the other thing you want to know is, if someone fully understands what you have to offer, if they bought your product, are they better off or buying your product or service than keeping their money? In addition to that, is that, you know, there's a lot of products that are for sale and people go, well, there's all kinds of trade-offs like cars, for example. You trade off gas mileage for towing capacity for cargo space for acceleration for styling, whatever it is, there's all kinds of trade-offs. So there's a room for everybody in the car market. There's a place for everybody. And the question is though, if everybody knows between you and all your competition, exactly what they have to offer, all their features, all their benefits, yours included, is there still people that will choose you? Is that based on price? Is it based on features or functionality or location and stuff like this? So you need to know if that answer is true, that you have something that there's a sizable market and can they, is there a sizable market for this type of product and do you have a place to fit inside of it? Now one of the ways that you can find out if there's a sizable market, if it sold something online, especially it would be easier, sometimes you're just doing research, is to check like Google AdWords, PPC, where it's pay-per-click campaigns and stuff like that. And what you're doing is you're checking to see how many searches a month are there out there for something. But you don't want searches that are based on keywords that anyone would search. So let's say for a second we were, someone was looking, let's say they were a baseball sports fan. We don't want their favorite team scores, favorite team player schedule, favorite team stats or anything like that. What you're looking for is, let's say they're selling, you want to sell baseball caps. You want to look for somebody that's typing in, some baseball team and baseball caps or memorabilia or collectibles or clothing. These are all things that people only type in when they're in the mood to buy something, not just that they type in the subject. You need to have the kind of words a person types in that differentiate them from someone that just wants information and someone that's typing in something that when they're looking to buy something. And when you check these, so there's numbers of searches a month in Google and places like that, but there's also the keywords. If they sell it online, if there's ads next to it, the competition and the price for those keywords for people that bid on to buy them, the higher that number that people are bidding to pay on it means there's probably a market there because people are willing to pay more money to compete and be advertised to prospects in that market. So you've got a number of organic searches and then keywords, keyword bidding competition on ad words and stuff like that. And also if you've got other places, but let me go on to a move on to the next subject. Okay, so we talked about our USP. What do you have to offer? That's called a USP or unique sales position. I didn't put a slide up there, but it's a very popular term. You shouldn't know it by now. If you don't, that's fine. But it basically just says, why should I buy it from you instead of somebody else? And you've got to have those reasons written down. Okay, so moving on, after we, after we, is there a sizable market, a loss, then we go into an opportunity for future sales. So J. Abraham terms this as lifetime value of a customer. Now this is some very important part. I know it sounds like this is not part of copywriting, but it really is. So the thing is, or else I wouldn't even bring this up. The thing is, is that lifetime value of a customer, if you know a person comes in your door, the average customer, some people buy one, some people buy forever, but on the average, what your average customer buys, if you know it when you get them in the door as a customer, how much the average person will spend, then you can spend more money getting that person onto your mailing list or onto your email list or into your store or whatever. And the thing is, if you have a competition, let's say you're selling, again, baseball caps and you've got a guy out there that's selling a baseball cap and he needs to turn a profit. And you find out that this guy, if you sell a baseball cap to somebody, you're going to get $500 out of them by the time the year is out, then you might be able to do what's called have a loss leader where you're losing money on the front end and you're underselling. You're selling the hat maybe even for less than you bought it for. And this just wipes out the competition if they're not thinking long term. They're going, well, this guy's selling it for less than I bought it for. How can I compete? So you can either beat them or make them pack up and leave and just have an edge. And when you sell it for less, you get something more valuable than the profit and the sale from that baseball cap. You've got a customer that he didn't get that you got because you sold it under, you took a loss on something in the beginning because you could make all this money in the back end, which is far more than the price that you lost on the hat or whatever it is that you lost. So not only do you get the same customers if you were competing on the same price, if you start competing for less or giving it away or when you probably don't want to give it away, but you're going to get all of his customers and they're going to end up on your list and it's going to be even worth that much more to you. So it's thinking a long term. It's thinking the long game, not the short game that makes part of this part of the plan. And a copywriter does spend his time when he's making these plans with the marketing, figuring out how this whole thing works together, how all the gears work. So he has to have that plan in his head along with his copy to know what he's doing, what he's working towards, what he's building up to. So this is a very important part of knowing this. So if you have a loss leader, and the thing is we're going to go into why the loss leader right now is kind of an important thing, but we'll get into it a few slides later. So when we're looking at this, and we've got these answers as a start of their market, is there an opportunity to feature sales? Now we want to do something else. Next we want to ask ourselves, who are you? Now when I talk about who are you, I don't mean that you're this person that loves their kids, you're good to your neighbors, you give to charity and do all this other stuff. Who are you in the sense of who are you to your prospective customers? So think of it if someone walked up to you and started to try to sell you something, and maybe they already know what you're interested in. The first thing you're going to say is who are you? Why should I be listening to you? And so we want to define more clearly and dimensionalize who you are, and we want to know what is your background? What did you study? Where were you born? Where were you raised? What did you grow up with? Were you raised with a baseball bat in your hands? And we'll get off the baseball analogy, there's just something off the top of my head. But the thing is, is we need to know what is your background? What are you an expert at? Did you train under somebody? Did you train other people? What is your story? Did you have a hard luck story? Did you have a, you know, did you triumph over something? You know, okay, and then how are you uniquely qualified to bring value to your prospect? And how can you prove it? Okay, do you have some way? Do you have, you know, any kind of proof, the social proof? Do you have certificates? Do you have degrees? Anything that you can do to prove it? Okay, next we want to know about your prospect. We want to know who are they? Similar questions. What is their background? What is their story? What are their hopes? What are their fears? If you notice something, we're getting into something else. We're getting into hopes and fears. What we're starting to do is go down an emotional path. We're going down the way people feel. Going further on, what are their desires? And what are the key emotions your prospect feels about what you have to offer? Okay? You want to get these all written down. You may know them, but you want to be staring back at you on a piece of paper. And another good way to do this is to have them on index cards. Okay, so moving on. Okay, when writing to your reader, the more you know about them, the better. You need to understand your prospects, and this is key. You need to know the story of your buyers. Okay. Let's take a little detour. I want to share something with you that really doesn't fit anywhere, but I thought it might. You might appreciate this. Okay, so remember I said we were finding out the lifetime value. Okay. There's another thing that's very valuable, and that's finding out what your audience has in common. Common other interests. Now I'm going to share with you a little secret. And the thing is, even if you don't know how to do this or just doesn't apply, it's this kind of thinking that will give you the kinds of answers that will really empower you. Okay. There's this thing very few people talk about. And what is called is called a co-op mailing list. And this can be a snail mail list or it can be an email list. Now what this list is, and this may or may not be for you, but this is a very powerful tool. What it is in the co-op mailing list is what it sounds like. You are cooperating with other people. And what you are doing is you're taking customers, people that are your customers who have bought something from you, and you're putting it into this database. But this database or this mailing list, this co-op mailing list is with millions of people. It has millions of people on it. And the thing is when you put this name in and say they bought this XYZ from you, the thing is you're probably not the first person to put their name in this pot. Other people have put this name in this pot. But the difference is you put this name in the pot saying they bought this thing from me. And what happens is, if those names have been put in there earlier, what happens is you get this cross-matching. And what it does is it tells you, oh, well, look at this. X percentage of the people that bought your product also bought this thing or did this thing. And what happens is you get these other interests. So let's say for a second, you put in a profile of customers and they were all buying four-wheel drive off-road parts from you. And all of a sudden you find out the co-op comes back and says, hey, you know what, these people all bought MMA Magazine. They all have mixed martial arts wrestling magazines that they buy from. Well, all of a sudden you have some new information for which you can work with. One of the things you can do is choose to sell subscriptions to MMA Magazine or whatever products they sell inside of it. Or let's say that they subscribe to MMA Magazine and they subscribe to off-road dirt biking, OK, magazine. And what happens is that you find that if your customers all have a whole bunch of different things in common, you can go out and even though you find that they have five other things in common, you can go, well, find all the customers that haven't bought from me that have all those five other things in common. And then I'll mail to them and try to sell them my thing. And this is target marketing. This will get you closer to your market. But what happens is this empowers you to be able to sell them other stuff, increase the lifetime value of your customer. So this is another powerful technique. Now I know I got off track. If it's no use to you, then it's no harm, no foul. But if you think this way, then you'll understand how you can expand and grow your business and grow your marketing and your selling and your sales volume based on this kind of thinking. So even if you don't use this type of technique, it's important that you think this way, OK? So we're going to get back. So then as we said, once you're sure what many of them have in common, you can offer them related products and services. OK. Now, as I said, you need to find out who your prospects are on a deeper level. That was a few slides back, right? Who they really are. OK. So for this example, take Playboy, for example. Playboy was created by a man who felt and knew deep down what his passion was. He was his own best customer. He knew he had an appeal in his product that some people could appreciate. The people who were his customers wasn't the biggest mystery. They were basically made up of any sexually mature male with a pulse. On the other hand, Playgirl was meant to appeal to women who wanted to visually appreciate the male body. But was that who bought Playgirl? Playgirl was on the newsstands for decades, so somebody was buying the magazine. But was it women? Over the years, Playgirl slowly came to terms with who their customers really were. I'm sure by now you've figured out that a large portion of Playgirl's customer base was gay men. Now, I want you to forget about for a moment whatever your feelings are towards gay issues. For now, I just want you to be a greedy SOB who owns Playgirl, whose only objective is to ring out every dollar out of the magazine as possible. Now, let me ask you. Knowing that the majority of your customers are gay, possibly make a difference in your bottom line. Would you change anything? Let me tell you how I would change the direction of the magazine, now knowing the truth about who my customers were. The first thing I would obviously do is cater to their desires. Next, I would create a bond with them, telling them that they don't need to feel ashamed or dirty about their desires. I would also add interviews with famous and successful gay icons. I would change the focus of the images, poses and styling, etc. to fuel their desires. This sounds like the top one. Fuel their desires sounds like cater desires. It's really different. Cater desires are desires that already exist. Fuel their desires is throwing gasoline on it. If I thought I could get away with it in the early days, I would put more than one guy in the pictures. Of course, all this is a moot point today as the magazine is dead. Even if I did all that, I couldn't possibly compete unless I took it online. I still have a hard time considering the exploding access to every variety of adult content online. I don't even think Playboy is profitable anymore on the stands. With so many competitors, it's so easy for the consumer to access a wealth of content that is very specific to their taste that a generalized magazine like Playgirl and Playboy doesn't really stand a chance. By the way, if I really wanted to go to the distance with the magazine, I'd also get political fighting for every gay cause I could possibly find. You have to do it, but I don't think it was for financial gain. I believe it was for his right to go through life on its own terms. Anyways, enough on that subject. Whatever position you think I have on the subject, just forget about it and shake it off. Like me or not, you are simply here to learn what I know. My father, Gary Halbert, believed you should be open to receive valuable information from anyone, including Hitler. Wherever there's good information, you should be willing to receive it and use it to your advantage. Okay, so moving on. Okay, where was I? Oh, yes. Okay, so, okay, deeply knowing deeply who your potential prospects and customers really are. So what was it? How did I? Oh, okay, this is just changing the subject. Sorry. I thought I was reading a sentence. So, deeply who your prospects and customers really are. Okay, so knowing this deep blur, you're in luck because it's now easier than ever to profile your target market. One way you can do this is to form a focus group, but I call these an FS group because you are paying them for their opinion and the people in the group feel an unspoken obligation to please you. And there's a much better way. You can patrol online forums. I first got this from Kevin Rogers and I've expanded on it for myself. He's a great way to do research on understanding your prospects and customers. And the thing is, when you do this, when you patrol these online forums, all you have to do is enter into Google your subject forum. So let's say Arthritis Forum or, you know, let's MMA Forum or just any forum you can think of that, you know, a wedding forum or anything that you want to think bridal that you think of. Okay. And what we're looking for are groups that are controlled by your focus or forums that are controlled by your prospects. Okay. What you're looking for is a dialogue that an exchange that's between the customers of the products and service, not between the customer and the person providing the product or service. Okay. This is where we are most likely to get frank and honest opinions on the subject and in their own words. And I can tell you this is absolute gold. And when I tell you, when we get into it later, you'll see what I mean. Okay. So don't just absorb this information. Start profiling your target market. You're going through the course. You might as well start filling in the blanks with your specific project. So, okay. And so I would like to say, when you're doing research, you're on a class. A quest to create a biography slash profile of the typical person who is most likely to buy what you have to offer. And this is what you're looking for. What are the biggest desires? The biggest fears. Remember, we talked about this before. We did, you know, if there's, if there's ones that aren't here that were previously listed before, these are what you, when you're in the forums, you want to make sure that you are not leaving the forum without these answers. You're filling in these blanks and writing these down so that they are staring back at you even passively when you're not thinking about them. Okay. And what do they believe and don't believe? One of the things we'll get into is talking about what they believe. Beliefs are different. Beliefs are not facts, but to the person that has the belief, they are facts. And you need to know that. And you need to know what people believe. Okay. What are the conspiracy theories? If they believe that the government is corrupt or fixed, then you need to know that. If you believe, if they believe in God or they believe they're an atheist or, you know, they believe in conspiracies or aliens or whatever it is, you need to know what do they believe. Okay. And what are their objections? You're going to hear this and you're listening for this. You got your, you got your stethoscope to the wall and you're trying to listen in on this conversation and it's very easy and it's, is it too expensive? Does it not fit? Won't work for them? Whatever it is that they have objections, you need to find out what they are and you need to be listening to them. Sometimes an objection is fine if a one person has an objection. If it's a common objection or an easily, or that's a misconception of objection, they don't understand that that is no longer a problem. And you need to know it and hear it and we'll talk about that some more. Okay. So moving on, who do they trust? Who are their leaders? Are they, are they political right? Are they political left? Are they religious leaders? Are they celebrities? Are they the town hero or whatever it is? Who do they trust? Who do they listen to? Who, you know, whose opinion do they value? What type of person is that? You know, and where are they at? Now, where are the at is very, very important in a lot of instances. Where they're at is if you're selling a solution or you're selling an offering, a benefit or a product or something, you need to meet the people that are ready to receive it and hear it and appreciate it and buy it. So you know, if, if you think about it as in stages of like school, schooling education, something that you sell to people in grade school is different than something you sell to somebody that's in junior high or high or college. Okay. And so you need to know where they're at. Okay. And that doesn't mean just education, wherever the person is at and they're trying to get to. So that brings up where do they want to be? So you need to, you know, if your prospects, you know, maybe you're selling a solution that gets someone to this kind of result or destination. Well, you're not going to sell the people already there. You want to sell the people that want to get there that you have the answer for them. So you need to, you know, and it's useless to talk to those people because the other people that have already gotten over because they've already solved that problem. So you want to address the people who are most likely in that moment in their life, in that stage where they've arrived at that point and there's the gap and you have the bridge to get some over. You need to start understanding where that is. Okay. So what do we have next? We have what are the words they use to describe their feelings? And this is where the forums will start to tell you things like that. You're going to hear them use lingo and you're going to hear them talk in certain ways. You're going to find out they talk in very basic, you know, crude ways or they're going to talk in very sophisticated ways or, you know, but you're going to find out how they talk and what dialogue they type of you and the words they use, the adjectives. How do they use the words to describe their feelings? Okay. And let me give you an example. I want to see what the next slide is just ahead of time. Okay, good. Okay. So what are the words that describe their feelings? Let's say for a second, you're selling something that deals with back pain and the person says, I wake up all day. I'm achy. I hurt. It takes me half the day before my back starts to loosen up and I can actually function and stuff like that. Okay, those are some of the words they may use. I'm achy, groggy, slow, tedious, whatever it is. And then there's this, what are the most powerful words that describe their emotions? That's where you take the words that they use and you find the synonyms that are more impassioned about it. So instead of waking up achy and pain, they wake up in excruciating, shooting, blinding pain and they suffer in agony all day and you need to know what those synonyms are. Those are more powerful versions of the words they use. Okay? And also, more powerful than the words they even use. So I'm moving on. Okay, by the way, one of the best things you can do to keep your finger on the pulse of your prospects and clients is be available to answer their questions. Ooh, their questions give you a lot of answers and a wealth of new ideas. It's like that old joke where a guy asks a psychiatrist, Doc, I've got this friend and he has this problem. Okay? So questions are full of answers. And another, I want to take a break here and share another tip with you. One of the things that made my father, Gary Halbert, so great as a marketer was that he started out as a classic door-to-door salesman and he sold classically encyclopedias. Okay? Now there is something that a face-to-face salesman gets and he learns and he learns it in a deeper level and that is he learns it when the turning point is. When you knock on someone's door and you try to sell them something, they're already resisting you selling them something. And you try to talk to them and what you're hoping on is some social courtesy that they won't be so rude as to slam a door in your face or something like that. And so a lot of times they're just waiting for a moment to turn you down and reject you. Now, the thing is when, and I've seen this firsthand when I've tried to persuade people to do things and it's a magical moment. There's a magical moment when you see a person and you can feel the resistance, you can see them cross their arms physically and mentally that they're not receptive, that they're resistive, you know, and when you start saying something, they might become indifferent. And then there's that moment where you say something to them and you see their arms like drop, their guard kind of drops down. And the thing is you can visually see that moment when they have stopped resisting. Now they're not sold, but they stop resisting. And one of the ways you know that things are starting to turn is they start to ask a question. And they don't ask a question to be nice or social or courteous. They ask a question because it's something of self-interest. And it's a moment to tell you that there's a chance that you might be able to sell them. And it, but more importantly is the piece of information. Whatever the question is, it promise you it's something of self-interest and it tells you what's important to the customer, the prospect. And so when people ask questions, there's always a selfish interest answer waiting for you if you just think about it. And it's not that hard to do. So just remember that. Okay. So I got probably ahead of my slides. So the questions are full of answers. They tell you outside of what you've already told them, what is most important to them. Okay. Now we're moving on to human nature. Okay. So we're just getting into some more of that's the human nature thing. Oh, well not more, but we're going into it. And this is a gear change. So we're going to go into human nature. And one of the things I want to ask you just up front, just to kind of be a thought little thought provoking and give you some obvious answers. Do you think it is easier to get a Republican to vote for a Republican or a Democrat? Or same question, to get a Democrat to vote for a Democrat or a Republican? And these answers are pretty obvious, right? How about getting someone to change religions or getting someone to believe in something or getting them to stop believing something? There's a reason the two no-nos in certain situations talk about politics and religions. It's because in some cases, like the ones just mentioned, is because people believe in what they believe in beyond all reason. And changing their beliefs and values and perspectives is nearly impossible. But if you could, at what cost? It's much easier to get in parallel with your prospects perspective of the world. And the more passion you have, someone has on a certain subject, the more influence you can have on them because they have a beyond reasoning emotion about it. The more emotional someone is, the less rational they become. Think about this. Can you remember a time when you or someone else was very emotional to the point of being beyond reason? How much level-headed reason did they have when someone died? People in that situation had a hard time trying just to function normally and get things done. How about when someone has fallen in love? Have you seen them make some of the worst decisions ever? How about this? Have you ever heard someone say, whatever you do, don't bring up blah, blah, blah to blah, blah, blah, because they will lose their mind? Or better yet, let's bring up sports fans again. The word fanatic fan is short for fanatic. The free online dictionary just defines fanatic as, let's get this slide going, a person marked or motivated by extreme unreasoning enthusiasm as for a cause. Sports fans lose their minds. They will take off their clothes in the dead of winter and paint their favorite team's name on their bodies. They will buy anything with their team's name on it. Whole stadiums have been known to break out in riots. And for what? There are few things that are truly less important than the outcome of a sporting event. Grown men will openly cry if their team loses anything from one little league game to a season championship. Think about it. Is any sport more important than you or your family or your friends' health and well-being? And yet you will rarely see some people as emotional as they are when participating or watching sporting events. If you understand how to trigger the key motions in someone and understand what direction they feel about it, you can propel them further in that direction towards what you have to offer. So, use this responsibly and sell what you truly believe in. And I mean that. That's honest. Tricking a reader is one of the most expensive things you can do. Some people spend a lifetime looking for backdoors and the ways of fooling their customers into buying something. Most of the time it takes the same effort to just give them what they really want. And it is immensely easier to sell them something a second time if you deliver them a satisfying result the first time. Not to mention, in most cases it's literally ten times harder to sell something to a new customer than it is to sell something to an existing, satisfied one. Connecting and bonding with your prospect. We're not changing gears again. Okay. Making a connection with your prospect has everything to do with, and we've already covered this, their story. Storytelling in the bigger picture is the most powerful selling technique and yet is relatively still a secret weapon in the larger universe of the marketing world. Remember I said if I'm going in with ten projects and I don't know what I'm going to write for, this is the technique I'm talking about. And we're going to get really heavy into it and you're going to understand and I'm going to prove to you this is the most powerful thing you can do in direct response copywriting. Okay. Okay. Let's see. And storytelling along with proof is one of the most powerful ways to turn your audience into true believers. They must believe in you. They must believe in how. They must believe it can be better. And they must believe it can happen to them. Okay. I don't know. I might be ahead of myself in the slides, but let me just take a moment here and say something. One of the things that people need is a new reason to believe. And the thing is if you look at somebody, a perfect example would be in weight loss. There's plenty of, you've heard of the blah, blah, blah diet and the blah, blah, blah diet and the blah, blah, blah diet. Okay. Right now, you know, maybe it's in the past, but the Atkins low carb diet is a thing. Someone who's bought a diet based on the Atkins low carb diet is probably and tried it and failed and didn't succeed is probably not receptive to buying another product that has done that, that is offering the same thing. Now as I mentioned, Thomas Hall was my father's biggest, my father favored him over all influences on teaching marketing that he was the biggest influence on him over Claude Hopkins, over Capels and he did love Robert Collier a whole lot, but Thomas Hall was the man as far as my father was concerned. And Thomas Hall wrote an ad and it was something to the effect of I lost X amount of weight using this Japanese, this diet in Japan. And here's why I think you can do it in America. And the diet was based, it was called the honey diet and it had something to do with just eating honey. But the point being is, is that a person that's tried and tried again, they're willing to try again in a lot of cases, depending on their desperation or their want or desire, hopes or whatever to, to get to some point. But they're not willing to try something again that didn't work. What they are willing to do is find a new reason to believe it can happen to them. Okay. If you have something where you have a skeptical or jaded or tired market, if you're going to try to go back into that market and offer them something, if you, if you can honestly give them a new reasoning and explain why you were, this, this, it can happen to them with this new way, this new different way that hasn't been done before, then you're in a lot better shape and you've got a powerful thing going on. Okay. Let's go into authority here. We want to talk about people. People now more than ever need more proof why they should believe you. And they are more resistant to finding that out by paying up front. So you have to reach out, prove yourself to them in the least resistant and most risk-free way possible. Okay. Here's a powerful technique. It's called proof, proof, proof, more proof, new proof. I've seen people do this before. I know Makepeace does it too. But a long time ago, there was a great fictional example of it, which was, there was a movie called Species. And in this movie, this alien, alien communication from space told the US government how to create this new creature. And why would any US government create a new creature? Now, I know this is fiction, but it still makes its point. And the point is, is that the person to explain away why they went ahead with this alien instructions on how to create this creature, this alien that was going to devastate the planet, was that previously the alien intelligence had sent them a formula for creating methane, for, with, it was free energy methane. So basically giving them free energy. And so this was proof. And then they went to straight to, well, if they did good on that, then this must be a great thing to do also. And so they created the alien. Okay. So another thing is, if you do show proof and all that, you know, when you show new proof, show honest, good proof, but it is a powerful technique. But if, so when trying to create trust with your reader, you know, you can do this and then give them facts that they already know, or it can easily confirm. And the more things you tell them that they already know to be true, the more likely they will believe you when you tell them something they don't know. Okay. And the thing is, um, trying to think, I lost my train of thought, like I promised I would. Uh, I guess I'll just keep moving on. And if I, if I remember, I'll share it with you. Okay. Now, now for another powerful tech, technique. All right. Give a new reason to believe why it's not possible. Remember I said it was a head unlike before. This is the, the, the honey diet. Now I want to give you another example of, of this. Um, I'm actually going to write it out two ways. The first sentence you see on the slide, it says, no wonder I couldn't achieve ABC. XYZ was standing in my way. But now this obstacle is no longer a problem. Here's another way of putting it. Okay. I just lost a slide. Basically. So let me fill in the slide. Um, um, I tried and I tried and no matter what I did, I couldn't push this car. Then I discovered the parking where it was on. Now I have every reason to believe I can do this. So I was missing a slide there. Um, but you get, you get the point. Okay. One sees that there was, they, they, there's a, they saw that the parking where Blake break was on that gives them all the hope and belief that this can happen. Okay. Now, um, and this is a thing. Another thing is like, if you're doing auto responder series or you're doing relationship building, um, or, you know, and this can be even in, we see this in book logs and MAGA logs. What you see is in book logs and MAGA logs, what, what the copywriters will do is they will give people valuable information they can put into action immediately. And what they do is they intersperse that with other information. They have to buy the product or the book or the report or anything to get it. So it's like, they'll give them some valuable information, then they'll give them an, they'll tell them about another piece of information they can, um, another solution they can obtain or have if they buy the book, then they'll, but they, they don't get the information right there and we'll go into that's bullets and blind bullets. But a bullet, you know, some bullets are, um, um, or some information is, is you just give it to them in your sales copy that is good and useful information. And then when you try to sell them other information, which you're holding out on the answer to, um, they're more likely to believe or, uh, that you know what you're talking about. So, and this is, this is key. The key here is not simply to give them something powerful. You must give them something they can appreciate. You can't give somebody a whole bunch of law books or a whole bunch of medical books or anything like that. Um, you, and you might be able to give them the kings of the kingdom, but if they can't appreciate it or it's too complicated, it will fall on deaf ears. Okay. All right. Okay. So I just want to get a bunch of those things kind of laid down and, and, uh, kind of out of the way, but not really. Um, and we want to move on to this, the copywriting preamble. After you figured out your market, after you figured out who they are, after you figured out who you are to them, after you figured out what you have to offer, you're now are almost ready to start writing copy. And we're going to go a little bit farther down this, by the way, but there are two more things you have to do. First is you have to, and this may sound silly or obvious, but you have to define what you want your reader to do. You want to, are you selling the click? Do you want them to watch your video? Do you want them to send you a check? Sign up for your email list in exchange for a free ebook or leave a comment on your blog or just start drinking your Kool-Aid. And in drinking your Kool-Aid just means they're, they're following you with, with a lot of allegiance and give you a lot of authority. And you can do this by, you know, give them a free book that's no strings attached that has some valuable content. And, you know, and as it says here, perhaps a free ebook with no squeeze. Okay. All right. Now, now after you've defined what you want the reader to do, specifically when I get to, when the person ends up getting to the end of my copy, this is what I want them to be ready to do. What you need to do is create your call to action. Okay. Call to action with specific instructions. And here you need to give your reader step-by-step instructions like enter your name and primary email address in the blue box below. And then check your email inbox or spam folder for an email with a subject line. Please confirm your subscription. Open the email and click on the link inside. Soon after you click on that link, you will be sent a second email with the most beautiful things inside. Very specific on what's going to happen to them, what they need to do, what actions. Here's another one. Call this toll free number 1-800-PAY-CASH-RITE-NOW. When you call, you will hear a recording of me saying, welcome to my awesome products order line. To get my latest creation at the tone, please enter your credit card number and expiration date and shipping address. And I will rush you awesome product number 3 and your free bonus. It's just that simple. So you're telling them actions, what they need to do. Also, my father was really good at saying, this is what you're going to experience. You're going to hear me say this. When you say welcome to my awesome products order line, and they call that line and they hear that exact script on that line or you tell them, then you'll see this and then you're going to get this. What happens is they see what you're telling them exactly in a visual almost way, experience what they're going to experience and that creates a trust and a believability that you are straight up and that you're being honest with them. Not only you're giving them instructions that help make the sale happen, but you're giving them a sense of security and trust because things are as you said they were going to be. Okay? So moving on. Next, we want to work your way backwards. All the way to the very beginning when they first encounter your first words in your headline or subject line. When they weren't even thinking about you. Okay? So keep in mind, you must keep this answer, the answer to this next question as the ultimate theme of your start to finish copy. And that is, what does my prospect have to hear in order for them to buy? Now I want to give you a lot more concepts you can keep in mind. So when crafting your masterpiece. So let's get to the 10,000 foot view of key concepts to keep in mind when writing your concept. I think that we're already into this 10,000 foot view, but let's do some more. So one thing is make it conversational. Okay? It's, you know, make sure it's, it's John Carlin would say in a me to you type conversation when writing to your reader, unless you're writing an advertorial from a reporter's perspective, you should almost never talk to them as if they are one in an audience of million. One of the most valuable words in copyright, powerful words in copyrighting is you. Okay? So when you, when this is a letter that sounds like you're just writing from me to you. That's it. You're not talking it from a corporation standpoint. We'd like to announce, you know, blah, blah, blah. Don't say I wanted to give my fans, my readers, everyone, unless you are a celebrity type or working on developing a sense of community. I just want to thank everyone for showing last night's webinar. It seems from the comments that everyone found it fun and informative. And that's, you know, when you're a leader of a group or something like that. And all of this, you can break the rules, but these are basic great things to follow. Okay. Speak to them at PSE, which PSE is plain simple English. You should never talk to them, anyone in any way that's above about a seventh grade reading level. Now, the thing is you should break that only if there are more sophisticated or complicated words that will better sell it. If you can sell it just as well using simple to understand simple sentences, no compound sentences and stuff like that, the better off you are. People don't, when they, if you think back on something great you read, you didn't think about how complicated it was written unless it was a piece of poetry or the Bible. You didn't think about how complicated it was. You just got the deep message and you remember that if you think about the stuff that, that moved you, you don't remember. So keep it simple, easy to understand. It's the concept that has the value, not the words that you used to describe it or not in that way. No BS, MBA terminology BS one would use in a boardroom presentation. Never use language that makes them feel you're talking above or down to them. Our dad once wrote a very offensive radio diet ad that took a very superior position in relation to its intended target audience that brought in a measly two orders. It might have been three orders, but it was terrible and all he did was spend his time insulting the prospect. Anyways, so moving on. Okay. Now it's important that you create a sense that you are one of them. Remember we're talking about storytelling. You are someone who's crossed over to the other side, a place where everyone inspires to be. Remember we talked about where they're at and where they want to be. Okay. And you haven't forgotten where you've come from and you are willing to reach back to others who are still where you used to be offering hand to help them cross over to the bright side. So you were starting to get, we're starting to frame up this storytelling. This is a very powerful technique and I'm going to show you in a second. I'm going to prove it. Okay. Okay. Right as if you're trying to convince your most skeptical prospect who will buy from you, but only if you make your case in the right way. But don't chase your prospects in a weak and needy way, begging them for the sale. Okay. Storytelling. Okay. Remember we're talking about story. Strumming their pains. Do you guys, you say I'm doing it, I'm violating, I should be talking as one of one. Strumming my pain with his fingers. Do you remember the bird of flax song? Okay. If you're old enough, you might remember it if you like listening to your parents' older music, strumming my pain with his fingers, singing my life with his words, killing me softly with his song, telling my whole life with his words. I felt that he found my letters and read each one out loud. He sang as if he knew me. Okay. This is a very powerful theme. This is a theme of what I'm trying to teach you. This song is this theme. There's a piece of lyrics. Now, I just pulled out the pieces of copy from this song, from the lyrics. There's a piece missing that should be in there and there's a line in the song that says, there he was, this young boy, a stranger to my eyes. The thing is, is that you need to understand is this is a story about a person who's never met the person up on stage before, but they feel so emotional and they feel almost embarrassed by the fact that this person is singing their story, their life so well. Okay. There's strumming. So, this is a very powerful, and this, by the way, I used to think this was a song that was written about Diamond. Turns out I looked it up at least on Wikipedia. It says it was really, it wasn't written by Roberta Flack. It was written by a songwriter who allegedly was watching Don McLean perform American Pie, which I find very interesting because this song, Strumming My Pain, is a very clear, very powerful piece of copy. In itself, the song itself, the lyrics in the song are a very powerful piece of copy, but it's singing about a other piece of copy, the Don McLean American Pie song, which is very unclear. It's very cryptic. And I find it interesting that someone would write so clearly about something that they were witnessing that was so enigmatic if you ask me, if you ever look up those lyrics. But the point here is this is, Strumming Their Pain is exactly the theme of the type of copywriting we're talking about. And we're going to cite some examples of people that have used this to make an ungodly sum of money. Okay. Okay. And so, along with this, okay, this is the thing. I always knew this concept because I've been surrounded since I was a little boy with the concept of this type of marketing and copywriting is that it didn't hit home until there was this friend of ours, Craig Clemens, and he was doing a presentation and he was going into this type of, well, this type of at least theme or overview that you would go to. And he was up on presenting to our mastermind group and he said during the presentation this, J. Abraham, he said, J. Abraham said, if you can describe your prospects pain better than they can, you will instantly create a bond with them. And think about it. That's someone who knows them. They're strumming their pain. They're singing their life, right? And this is a person that's been there, okay? And by the way, I asked J. about this and he said it sounded like something he would have said. By the way, this is one of the most powerful insights I've ever heard. Like I said, I knew the technique but it took on a new meaning when I heard it described in this way. And like I said, I know it's powerful because it's used in some of the most widely successful sales copy in history. Okay, it's used in Ben Suarez's Seven Steps to Freedom, Big Winner Add. Gigantic. It's used in Joe Carbo's The Lazy Man's Way to Riches. Big Winner Add ran for I think somewhere around 18 years, sometimes twice a week as a full page ad in newspapers. And it was used in our dad's, Gary Halberts, The Amazing Diet Secret of a Desperate Houseway from Ohio. And a whole lot more. There's a whole bunch of ads that he wrote based on this exact theme. Okay, so moving on. So when stubbing your pain, their pain, you want to hit them with their biggest wants, desires, and frustrations. Remember that's why we're getting that info. You want to make them feel the strongest emotions associated with those wants, desires, and frustrations. People are much more open to hearing from people who are like them. Like we said, strumming or pain. You have been where they've been, where the reader is right now. You can make a strong connection with your prospects if you know the emotional highs and lows they identify with. Something they joyfully or painfully know all too well. It is best if your story is their story. I hope you're getting the steam. It's all coming together. Alcoholics want to hear of a way out by alcoholics. People who have been abused as children want to hear from Oprah. And overweight people want to hear from someone who's tried and failed again and again. Not from some skinny bitch who drinks five cafe lattes a day and has never faced the cold reality of no longer fitting into her favorite outfit. Okay, did the word bitch shock you? Do you think it is powerful and emotional? Do you think someone who is overweight might have strong thoughts like that? Do you think they might appreciate a friend who helps them throw stones at their frustrations? In every piece of copy there is a group of people you might alienate, but the others will gravitate to you just that much more. Just think politics and religion. You have to use your own judgment on when it's time to use something like that. But I wanted to shock you. I didn't want you to know it was coming. Okay, just make sure the music you are playing is what they are already listening to. So that's why we are listening to them or listening to how they talk. We want to hear their pains, the words they describe their pains, everything else. And it's best if, like we said, your story is their story. Okay, it's one thing to place yourself in the reader's shoes. It's another to have them mentally return to a place they know all too well, especially recently and especially if it was a very emotional experience. A vivid looking back story that goes farther back than where the reader is now or just to where they are presently that ends up at their dream destination is a very powerful formula.
