Let's get back on track. Okay. In a lot of cases, it's a good idea to leave the reader feeling incomplete, but they can feel complete if they click on the link. Keep in mind it's important to be very conscious of the frame of mind the reader is in when they open your email. Sign up to your list. Okay. So, if the sign outside the store says incense and things, but when they enter they realize you are an auto parts store, there's going to be little overlap of interest and more likely a feeling of resentment and lack of trust that wasn't there before. Subdivision lines can also serve as, ooh, where did I, I don't know, I lost something. Okay. Oh yeah. No, that's right. Subdivision lines can also serve as a shark in the water. This is kind of cool. Okay. So, James Spielberg kind of got lucky when the mechanical shark broke down during the filming of Jaws. In some scenes he just shot a detached floating dock being towed through the water without any evidence that there was in fact a shark in the water. Just the suggestion of one. What everyone realized was that the shark and everyone's individual imaginations were much scarier than anything Hollywood could ever dream up. In the original movie Alien, the alien only peeled four times, but only briefly. So the point is, it's the not knowing in a lot of instances that is more powerful in people's minds than the reality. The same is true for positive feelings. When Howard Carter drilled a hole through the plaster seal of King Tut's tomb, Lord Kamar Vans, I can never read that right, Carter's expedition sponsor asked him if he could see anything to which Carter replied, yes, wonderful things. Can you imagine what you, if we didn't know it was in King Tut's tomb now, can you, you're imagination could do crazy stuff. Okay, sometimes the imagination can fill in the blanks better than the reality. And the reader can definitely define in their own mind what wonderful means to them. This way of having the reader fill in the blanks for themselves, customizing a vision in their own minds has proven a very effective in sales copying written for business opportunity to offers. Bizops, bizops are a little bit different. Our bizop customers are people that are looking for a shortcut. And as my father would say, once people find out there's some work they have to do, the whole magic is worn off. So you see a lot of business opportunity copy that's written without the people actually knowing what the business is before they buy. So, so there is, you know, when it comes to bizop, they'll say, well, there's no none of this and you don't have to do that. You don't have to stuff envelopes. You don't have to make any outbound telephone calls. You don't have to do this. You don't have to do that. And they tell you it's wonderful and it brings you this and it brings you that, but they don't tell you what it is that you have to do. So bizop, I don't know, I'm not a fan of it. But anyways, moving on. So more importantly, it works great for headlines. And in the case of emails, sub declines. Let me explain. I once wrote a sub decline that said this. This is every blanks second worst nightmare. Not only does the sub decline carry curiosity, it starts the imagination filling the blank. Not only for the second nightmare, but that there is also a list and they wonder what the first is. And the answer to both are contained inside the email. A very famous version from long ago was a newspaper ad that had this headline. Do you make these mistakes in English? The key word in the headline is these because it promises that the answer to something they can't possibly know is contained in the body copy. The other version of this type of headline is often using PPC, pay per click ads, where the key word in the headline is this. Blank discovers how do you benefit statement with this one weird trick. If you've seen this around, you know what I'm talking about. Okay. Now there is a time when you are writing copy where you're going to have to think for yourself and know when it's better to use curiosity headlines and when it's better not to. For example, if you're selling life insurance, there's a good chance you're going to want to use curiosity to draw your reader in. Whereas if you've discovered the cure for pancreatic cancer, the headline needs no curiosity the element to get your target prospect to listen up. If you can't make a straight offer out of the gate, if the reader needs to be romanced a little more, if they need to be better educated as what you have to offer and how it differs from their pre pre-precisee bias, then there's a good chance you're going to have to find an alternate approach than straight on. Okay. Let's get into body copy. Your body copy must continue where the subject line left off. It's important your headline continue into the body copy and sets the read up with a big payoff. We talked about this. If you are trying to finesse their thinking and need to lay down some groundwork and establish a new perspective slash positioning in the reader's mind about how their preconceived knee jerk responses towards what you have to offer, if you ever feel like making a straight offer to your prospect, we'll make them quickly tune you out where you feel like saying, wait, you didn't let me finish. Then you're probably need a more sophisticated sales process. Now we've already kind of assumed that that we're going to go down the storytelling that we're going to go down a sophisticated sales process. We're not going to go gasoline seven bucks a gallon or Hass avocados, two for $3. By the way, this will be the reality for almost everyone. If you are creative and even though you aren't immediately paying off the curiosity you injected in the reader's mind with your subject line, you will be able to start your body copy with a varying in dynamic message that they can enjoy reading all the way through. In other words, they will enjoy it from the very beginning, not only the destination, but also the journey. Okay, we're really shifting gears again. We're going into analogies and metaphors. These are things you may or may not use. In this particular case, I did use it in the copy I ended up writing after creating this slideshow. Okay, analogies and metaphors in copyright are used for two main purposes. The first is to simplify something complicated and make it memorable. The second is to create more drama. If you have analogies, you can use analogies. A lot of analogies use like a car because the car has all this interworking. It's its own system. It's an internal system, but it also goes down the road and it fits a lot of ways of explaining things in life. It will explain electricity using water and flow and pressure and stuff like that. It's anything that you can take complicated and you can make simple for the reader that it will help you a lot if you have a complicated subject and creating more drama. One of the great analogies or metaphors I heard was I saw in a movie, this guy was feeling lost and frustrated and he just said, I felt like a dog on a freeway. That was a very good analogy. Anyways, all right. Next we're moving, I think we're on questions. Questions are a very powerful tool in copyrighting. To ask questions. The reason is because it makes the reader customize their experiences. Like those fashion magazines that have quizzes in them. Like quiz, how romantic is your man or quiz, what is your sexual intelligence? All of a sudden the reader is able to customize the experience to who they are and where they fit in relationships with the rest of the world. Have you ever felt alone in a crowd? Have you ever felt ignored in high school or college growing? Those things, was there ever a moment when this, when your two girlfriends made you feel left out? Things like that. Whenever you ask those questions, questions that almost everyone's experienced or your target market is the dominant common experience. When you ask those questions then you're putting the reader into the story. You're setting them into the scene. They become emotionally invested in what you're writing to them. Moving on. Questions can make them feel insecure, unsatisfied, understood, inspired and everything in between. The most important it is questions that make the reader mentally self-qualify themselves for your product and services. If you're already making over 15% interest on your money then this isn't for you. Well that sounds like a negative qualification but unless a reader is just going to go, man I wish I had that problem. So the questions can mentally qualify the person. One important thing is not to ask the prospect question to which there is any chance the answer could be no. One way to avoid this is to not be too specific in details of the question. Where only a few people have had that specific kind of experience. Also but just on the opposite side of that, don't be afraid to ask a question to which you will lose readers who will never buy what you're selling no matter what. Sometimes those questions will bring the other readers in closer. Here's a great example of some questions. Some pharmaceutical companies, they do this. They go, do you ever feel tired, restless, agitated, loss of appetite, anxious? These ads are looking for you to match yourself to one or more of these symptoms. The more the better. The more you might need X. These are self-qualifiers. Oh yeah, that's me. Oh I need Xenotrax, whatever medication. So next one moving on to what's called the if then statement. It's a relative of the question. If you've ever felt like your children never listened to you, then my new LLNLP training course is perfect for you. I got this from somebody in our mastermind that had something similar to this. But you see the if then. You're qualifying yourself. If you ever felt like this, then this is where you have the readers self-qualify themselves and have them believe the product is right for them. Now we're moving on to objections. No matter who your prospects are, a significant number of them have objections. It's very important you know what the common objections are. You must bring up the objections and explain them away. They aren't going to go away just because you don't want to talk about them. You don't want to bring them up. And you're going to lose a lot of sales in the process. One of the best ways to overcome objections is to know when the objection appears in the reader's mind. My father was brilliant at this. Many times it comes up at a precise moment when they are reading your copy. I'll show you a small objection killer that I use in the copy later on. The time to address their objection is immediately after you trigger it in their mind. This often occurs when you bring up a particular feature or benefit in your product or service. Did you notice something very powerful in what I just said? I'll repeat it again. The time to address the objection is immediately after you trigger it in their mind. This often occurs when you bring up a particular feature or benefit in your product or service. I'm being redundant. Sorry about that. One of the disadvantages of copywriting is no matter... Okay, I can't read today. One of the disadvantages of copywriting is no matter if it's an email, a video, or print, it's a one-way conversation. What I mean by this is... Oh, it's a one-way conversation, right? So we know it's a one-way... Only you get to talk. But one of the advantages of ad copy is that it's a one-way conversation. You are in complete control of the direction of the conversation. But only as long as the reader remains interested. That means if you plan a big enough anchor in the reader's mind, you will be able to get your complete message out. The reader doesn't have a chance here to be argumentative with you. If you plan a big enough hook, you want to keep it as interesting and agreeable all the way through as you want. Some people are just having an argumentative nature, and if you try to talk to them about something that will entertain themselves with frustrating you or doing whatever they want to do. But when you have something that's interested in them and they want to hear that story and they want to go down that road, that's where you can start to soften their position up if they're a resistant type of reader. Moving on. If they want the payoff, they're going to have to hear you out. Now we're moving into PSs, post-scripts. Thing about... PSs are one of the most read pieces of copy. Many people automatically skip to read the bottom line. PS is often used like a traffic cop to wave the reader back up into the copy. So let's say the reader decides... So let's say the reader starts skip reading and go straight to the PS. But the reader opened your email because it had a subject line that said, I used to suffer from chronic back pain until I accidentally discovered a strange antique in my grandmother's attic. And now they're skipping down to the PS, which says, PS, who would have thought such a cheap little toy would be the ultimate answer to so many people's back problems? As you can see, we didn't give the reader any answers, we just fueled the curiosity even more. The PS is more often used to synopsize the main selling points of the letter. PS, if you order within the next 48 hours, you not only will get the full set of 14 precision German professional chef knives, you also get the Laser Razor Blade Sharpener, all for the low introductory price of $9.95. When it comes with our ironclad, no questions asked, lifetime guarantee. And a lot of people will skip even this and all they'll do is talk about the powerful benefits. So use a PS that just has powerful benefits in it alone, where you don't even know what the product is or restate the offer. So there are several techniques you can use in PSs to help reinforce your sales. And here's another piece of advice. Every great copywriter does this. And it's have people just like your prospects read your copy before you share it with the world. Now, that's your ideal. If you can do that, that's great. But I know every copywriter I've ever known has their closest, they have their wives, their girlfriends, their mothers, their brothers, everybody read their copy before they share it with the world. You should always do that. That's when it helps you find out questions, things that you're so close to, you couldn't even figure out yourself. Okay, so it says here, some people won't reach out and ask you about what's unclear to them about your copy. Many of them will just stop reading. And that's a killer. So if one person is unclear about something, there is probably others just as lost on that point. But you will never know unless they can ask you. Okay, now we're getting into some mechanics. This is just mechanics. This isn't... Okay. Type. When writing on online copy, use sans serif fonts like these. Okay. This is sans serif. Sans serif. The serif are those little dots and little tails and things that are in a lot of fonts. Okay. And they have, I guess you call them curlicues on the tips of the letters. Okay. So sans serif is without the curlicues. These are straight, almost block-like type letters. Okay. But the... So without the serif, without the curlicues. Now with the serif fonts, they have these little... Like if you look at the F in the serif, right? That's in the letter F in the word serif. You'll see that it has like a bulbous tip to it. And you see on the T's on the fonts, they have these hooks to them and stuff like that. And you have these little accents on the T's and all the capital letters and the feats and everywhere. And the thing is, the reason for this, that you use a different one, is if you're going to use... If you're going to do online text, you want to do the font set that's above. If you want to do printed text, you want to do usually, in some cases, the font text that's below. Okay. The reason for this is because serif fonts are easy to read and print because of two reasons. Printed copy is usually a very high resolution and also because printed type is rendered using dots. Okay. On the other hand, computer screens render their images using squares. So imagine if you have a bunch of square tiles, like, let's say, like scrabble tiles. Okay. Just looking at them on the blank side of them. And someone tells you to create a circle laying out the squares. No matter how you do it, the circle will come out with jagged edges. Now those jagged edges will appear less jagged, the larger the circle or higher the resolution of the screen. Okay. I'm trying to read this. So, and the thing is with computers, computers render things called pixels, stands for picture elements that are squares. So drawing circles with squares is a whole lot harder on a computer screen where the resolution is higher. Okay. So I'm looking through the notes. Okay. Okay. Yeah. And, you know, I noticed here it says, you know, computers will do some tricky interpolation. Turpolation is where you start to make some grays and stuff around things so that it's not a complete square. It's kind of looks a little blended. It's not important, but at least a muddling and blurring of the type. Okay. So I'm going to copy that will ultimately read online is to use the upper type. I have one, the sometimes people associate serotype with credibility of newspapers if they grew up. So if you're writing to an older audience or something and subconsciously or not, it can give you credibility by association to some of those readers. Okay. We get credibility and some of that stuff by doing what are called avatars if you were back in print. If newspapers were still the big thing that advertorials are ads that look like they're a story or an editorial and they get five times the readership. So I want to talk about getting five times your return on investment. Advertorials are the way to go. But anyways, I'm babbling on. Okay. Now we're moving into paragraphs. Oh, that's got reformatted. Reading online is harder on the eyes and brain. You have to make it easier on the reader. Unfortunately, unlike printed copy, we don't really have a limit on how long or how many pages our copy takes up. Okay. Because of this, you can give the reader mental and physical eye relief by creating short one and two sentence paragraphs. Now this is a one sentence paragraph, but that's a long sentence. So I'm guilty of violating it. Also you don't want to make your copy go all the way across the screen. This creates eye fatigue and the reader can easily lose track of what line they were on when the eye goes back to the left to read the next line. It's kind of some obvious stuff. Like I said, it's just mechanics. Images include images whenever possible. Now this is stuff that I've learned from Big Jason and it's proven out to be true in all the tests that we did on emails and stuff like that. Images really increase your click through rate. Okay. So if you can, if your copy can use it, use images when you can. And here's some of the things. Make the images a link. So not just the text, but it's a big target. They click on it and the cursor turns into a hand. They know it's going to take them somewhere. They might just click on it just that much more. So also using one pixel border. Some people will take their emails and we're just getting any emails right now is that they'll don't want any images to load. Maybe they're on an old DSL modem or they're on their phone and they don't want to use up all their bandwidth loading images that they have to pay for with the overages and stuff. So with one pixel border, what it does is it makes a space because if there's a picture that it won't load, that their browser won't load, it'll collapse it and they'll never know there's a picture there they're missing seeing. But if you create a one pixel border, it will keep the paragraph separated and they'll see a one border frame knowing that there's a picture they're not seeing. And in addition to that is make sure to use a curiosity alt text. Now if you type in, if you put an image into say an HTML message, now this is maybe for a tech guy, somebody who works for you if you're not tech savvy. But basically when you put an image into an email or if you put it into any just web page at all and they don't load images, what happens is if the browser knows that images are turned off, what it does is it automatically shows some text that you write as alternate text, alternate meaning, since you're not going to show the image show this text instead. And so you can say turn on images, check out my funny mug in a Sabral hat, writing in donkey and whatever. And the thing is you can use this curiosity to get them to turn on the images and see the images and fuel their curiosity more and get them to click on your link. It's just as simple. Salutations, most cases you want to use the salutations using someone's name in an email in the subject line or in the first part of the body copy. Just signals that you know them just a little bit better than a generic spammer. You know something about them. They somehow they got their name. Maybe you put your name on a list. And I think using last names is only good in some cases because it's not a friendly, no one refers to the salutations to their friends by using their first and last name. They usually just use their first name. It's a little more informal, a little more personal. But there's times when you don't want to use it where it might appear divisive to use the name and so you have to use your judgment on when you should use it. So I just said that in most cases just use the first name. Now we're getting into bullets. This is a very important part. My dad and John Carlton are big proponents of my dad wrote a single ad that was just basically all bullets and it was a free website reveals and it was just bullets, bullets, bullets, bullets. And bullets are these short little benefit or curiosity elements that you put in there. Usually one sentence, maybe two. So this is the thing about bullets and they're very valuable. And the thing is bullets are something you normally use because other parents and the physical parents of bullets, bullets, let people usually know that you're selling something. But that's fine. A lot of times you're going along with a relationship with people and they get to know you and they start drinking your Kool-Aid. You give them valuable information. They grow like, get to know, like and trust you. And when you come to make an offer for them, they just want to know about it. And so they do want you to put your best foot forward so that they know what you're offering to see if they really want to buy it and you want to give them those bullets. But if you're doing an avatarl or you're trying to make something look like a personal letter, you don't want to give away that this is a sales letter. You want them to find that out after you've already kind of romanced and warmed them up. A little foreplay is involved here. So bullets are your chance to take free swings at your prospects hot buttons. Bullets are the sound bites of your product or service features and benefits. This is your chance to back up the truck and dump the whole load on your prospect. Think of it like a bookstore or an ice cream shop where a person, for the most part, doesn't care that you carry products that are of no interest to them. They just care that some of your products touch them in their warm and fuzzy places. Don't get me wrong, I'm sure there is a bullet you could write that could stink up the whole party but I can't help you with that. You're just going to have to use your noggin. So after I read that, let's move on. Okay, I think I went too fast. Oh yeah, I went too fast. Let's do this. Okay, so let's get to this next bullet. I lost my train of thought there. Okay, these are bullets. The thing is, this is what a bullet looks like. It's just a bullet and this is what they look like. And one of the things you want to do is put a space between the bullet and the first letter of the bullet you're writing. This would be a sentence you say. Okay, just losing my space here. Okay, so when putting a list of bullets in your copy, you want to alternate bolding each bullet. Okay, and when pre-space permits, like with online copy, you want to put a space between each bullet like this. Ah, that slide messed up. Okay, but you see the difference. So the bullet number three would have been tabbed in a couple of times. When making space between bullets, you usually get an empty line. You can eliminate the bullet on the empty line by just clicking backspace. Then you go down and manually insert your cursor on the end of the next line, hit enter, and then backspace. And then you will be able to eliminate the bullets on the empty lines. You will create a lot of eye relief when you put a space between the bullet and its sentences and also put a line between each bullet and bold every other bullet. Where's that one slide didn't go like that? Okay, blind bullets. Blind bullets simply tease the reader with a benefit they can't have unless they buy the product like this. Eat this delicious fruit and you'll never have to floss again or like. These three little words you can say that will make her instantly XYZ. Okay. Okay. Oh, this is... All right, we're getting to the end here. We're getting into something I really want to share with you. Okay, now I have a treat for you. I see people get off topic in their copy all the time and I make that mistake also. Okay, so this is a technique I used to make sure I stay laser focused on a straight line of thought. Okay. It was originally meant for fiction writers, but you can use this too. It's called the Hemingway Challenge. Ernest Hemingway was once challenged to write a story in six words. This is what he came up with. For sale, baby shoes never used. Okay, the point here is to take an extremely lean and efficient story and only expand on it in ways that make the story stronger and nothing more. If it doesn't add to the power of the story, it shouldn't be in the copy. Now I'm going to contradict myself here because in the creative process, you need to have the creative freedom to let your mind spill your guts all over the page. And then after taking a day off, go back and clean it up. This is a very common technique and it works great. Okay. But I'm not really contradicting myself because copy isn't usually written at the keyboard. It's written in the mind before you even sit down. At least the six word story is, along with the theme and some way points, you can plan on hitting in your copy. So basically you come up with your six word story, your theme, tone and angle, and your way points. With those, you now have a rough map from beginning to end. Okay. Then with this stuff in mind, you go and spill your guts on the page. Keep in mind that the dachshund must collect along the way, weaving a path like a drunk taking a sobriety test. Then you go back in and yank it back into a straight line. Okay. The Hemingway challenge is a great exercise. Use it if you think it will work for you. If you try, you can figure out your lean, efficient power pack theme for your copy. For example, Desperate Ohio Housewife's amazing diet secret. Six words. Or, started playing piano, everyone quit laughing. Or nerd gets goddess to fall madly. Or the lazy man's way to riches. Seven steps to freedom. Sing in my life with his words. The point is to take this extremely lean, efficient story and expand on it in ways that make the story stronger. Okay. Oh, I've already read that. I've got a slide that's been copied. Okay. So, moving on. Okay. Now, what happened next was, this is where I got to, when I got to the end of the slides and I decided I was just going to crack open a yellow pages and go through every single page and see what products and services people were offering. And I'm going to go back and forth a couple of times for this. I want to give you a chance to see why. Ask yourself, why do you think I chose these subjects as things that for the foundation that I just laid out for you as to what, why I would choose these ones to be the ones if I had to write some selling copy for it. Now, don't cop out. If you have a, you know, your CPA and you sell a business, trust me, you can still do this storytelling. Claude Hopkins did it with a beer company. And what he did is he went in, I think it was Schlitz and he took them from like dead last to number one by telling a story about how they, how they make their beer so pure. And what they, what everyone didn't know was this was the brewing standard that everyone followed. But when Claude Hopkins went in and spelled it out loud, everyone says, hey, we do that too. But the thing was no one had told the story to the readers. And so when everyone tried to, tried to come out and say the same thing, it was already too late. They just looked like imitators. I forget who told me that, but it's a very valuable story as far as this point is concerned. Okay. So we got education, tutoring, public speaking, dating, golf. Think about why I would choose these money, investing, management, bankruptcy, credit repair. Okay. Medical slash aesthetics, hair replacement, cosmetic dentistry, cosmetic surgery, hair styling, testosterone replacement therapy. Okay. We're getting into contracting, plumbing, electrical, general, and mold remediation. Safety, poison, lead, asbestos, self-defense, daycare, pests, home security, alarm systems, locksmith. Okay. This is the thing. Kind of gave it away, but let's do it here. Why do you think I chose these? I'll tell you what the reason I chose these particular subject matters is, is because all of these subjects, if you look at them, they all have the potential for a death or glory story. They have a high contrast role. People can either come out feeling victorious or they can feel devastated by the type of events that follow these types of subjects you could sell for. Okay. So the thing is, which one do you think I chose? I can tell you, when I thought about the one that could have the greatest story and have the greatest contrast of outcome for a good, for a good result or a bad result or a bad story was, I chose cosmetic surgery. Okay. Think about it. That could go terribly wrong. Think about the price of it going terribly wrong. Think about that could go terribly well and think about the glory of it going wonderfully well. Okay. So this was my, I'm coming up with it, my Hemingway challenge. No surgeon becomes client, clients raving. That should give you a strong clue as to how this thing is going to be laid out. Now the other thing is, one of the other things which I took off of the slideshow, I don't know why it disappeared, but the thing is, one of the other things I would have chosen as one of the most death or glory moments, even though it's not, I mean, you could really go into medicine and cancer and stuff like that that can be really heavy. But one of the other things I would have gone into would have been wedding planners. That story, you could see things going great or going terrible and it's got a great, it could have a great story element. You could find that all over forums and stuff like that. But in any case, all of those subject matters really are great stories. And the thing is, if you think about anything, if you think about something, someone has to do. If their roof is leaking, it's something they didn't want to deal with, but now they have to deal with. And now they're in the Yellow Pages and they have emotional experience with it. Maybe boring or dry to some people, but to a person that's faced with having to hire somebody to do that, that is an emotional experience and it has a story behind it. So anything a person has to get, I mean, whether it's dog training, if their dog's chewing up their house and making a mess of the place and biting people and not protecting the family, there's all, whatever it is, even though it's small, when a person pulls up in the Yellow Pages, they are feeling something emotional about it. And that's true about almost all buying decisions. When they're involved, they want it to go well. So anyhow, now we're going to move on.
